topic: Firmament Scaling Function from V12 geometry — post-EXP-266 editorial cleanup (stency fragment removed)
change_summary:
EXP-266 integrated: single substring replacement in detail_evidence — 'nature.stency.

' → 'nature.

'. Net delta: −7 chars (length 2197 → 2190). No other field touched. Verified post-patch: substring 'stency' appears 8 times across wins.json, all as part of the legitimate word 'consistency'; no leftover fragment remains. Verdict, finding, detail_claim, detail_verdict_text, both TLDRs, code_analysis, detail_extra all unchanged.
our_argument_summary: The decider has fully resolved the c4-flagged narrative inversion: detail_evidence now leads with version-instability (HAD 1.47 V51 vs 1.89 V50.6, BOU radius 5,715 → 9,983 km), names the V12-derivation self-contradiction explicitly, cross-references Section 1.8 coordinate-collapse as a second structural contradiction, and the c4-flagged paragraph duplication is gone. EXP-266 is a clean cosmetic patch on top of that already-good rewrite — it removes a 7-character 'stency.' fragment that was a partial-replace artefact (left over from an earlier 'consistency'-related edit). The Self-Contradicted verdict is well-earned: a formula whose output swings 28% at HAD when the author revises non-V12 parameters between dome versions cannot also be a stable derivation from fixed V12 geometry. The author must concede either that V12 is wrong, that V51 is a re-parameterization not a derivation, or that one of the two version sets is mislabeled. Each concession is itself a contradiction with the detail_claim. Remaining holes are minor: a unit-omission inside the technical-source paragraph (BOU radii cited without 'km' in one position even though the lead paragraph uses 'km'), a pre-existing transliteration imprecision (the dome publishes ΔZ, not ΔB), and 'purely geometric' slightly overstates the FSF — H(r) embeds the aetheric-medium refraction parameters n_0, n_∞ — though the parameters cancel substantially in the ratio.

relevant_files: raw-text/03-predictions.txt (FSF table line 21–34: explicit delta_Z = baseline x coverage x FSF)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: FSF = (H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU). Per-station predicted ΔZ = baseline × coverage × FSF, with two baselines published (BOU −10.9 nT, W004 −22.24 nT) and ±~2 nT tolerance per station. Published FSFs (EBR 1.029, HAD 1.471, LER 2.075, NGK 1.529, CLF 1.325, SNK 0.642, COI 1.008, SPT 1.001, ESK 1.722) reproduce ~3% under V51 ellipse H(r) + V51 station radii; do not reproduce under V50.6 exponential H(r) + V50.6 radii (HAD computes 1.89 vs published 1.471).
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Hybrid. Published per-station FSF table is hardcoded in raw-text/03-predictions.txt; the formula is computed in monitor.py Domain 10. The parameters that reproduce the published table are V51-specific.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Dual baselines (BOU vs W004) ≈ 2× spread on ΔZ window plus ±~2 nT tolerance bands. Wide observational window will dilute eclipse-day verdict. Separate from version-instability self-contradiction.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Two pre-existing imprecisions in detail_evidence (carry-overs from c4, NOT introduced by EXP-266): (1) detail_evidence calls the formula 'ΔB = baseline × coverage × FSF' but raw-text/03-predictions.txt line 21 explicitly publishes it as 'delta_Z = baseline x coverage x FSF' — ΔZ is the vertical component of the geomagnetic field, not the total magnitude; (2) detail_evidence says 'BOU shifts from r=5715 (V50.6) to r=9983 (V51)' without 'km' units in the technical-source paragraph, even though the lead paragraph uses '5,715 km' and '9,983 km'. Neither materially affects the self-contradiction argument.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: wins.json currently tags this 'none'; the c4 review argued for 'hardcoded' but flagged it as low-urgency until eclipse-day data populates the observation slot. EXP-266 does not touch the tag and we should not edit it here. The eclipse window is 2026-08-12; the analyst can re-tag once observed values arrive.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard physics (Sq current suppression — Takeda & Yamada 1989; Curto et al. 2006) predicts station-amplitude variation with magnetic latitude and eclipse path geometry. The dome FSF = (H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) is a dome-geometric ratio with no standard analog. Causal-confound risk exists (FSF may incidentally correlate with magnetic latitude) but that is a separate critique, not relabeling. 'false' correct.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: E001–E009 published before the eclipse, observations not yet available. Self-contradiction is structural (parameter-set sensitivity), not retrodiction. 'false' correct.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: FSF derives mechanically from H(r) and station radii; the formula does start from dome parameters. The version-instability shows the derivation is parameter-set-specific, but the path from dome geometry → number exists. 'true' correct. The detail_claim asserting V12 derivation is what conflicts — that is the verdict-grounding contradiction, not a tag flip.

holes_found.description: Unit inconsistency inside the technical-source paragraph: lead paragraph cites 'r = 5,715 km (V50.6)' and 'r = 9,983 km (V51)' with 'km' units, but the technical-source paragraph at the bottom restates the same shift as 'BOU shifts from r=5715 (V50.6) to r=9983 (V51)' without units (and without comma thousands separators). Inside the same field, the same number is rendered two different ways. A defender reading top-down sees consistent km units; a defender skimming the bottom paragraph sees a unitless integer that must be inferred from context. Pre-existing — NOT introduced by EXP-266.
holes_found.recommendation: Trivial edit: replace 'BOU shifts from r=5715 (V50.6) to r=9983 (V51)' with 'BOU shifts from r = 5,715 km (V50.6) to r = 9,983 km (V51)'. Brings the second mention in line with the first. Not blocking; can be folded into a future minor editorial pass.

holes_found.description: Citation imprecision: detail_evidence transliterates the dome formula as 'ΔB = baseline × coverage × FSF'. raw-text/03-predictions.txt line 21 publishes it explicitly as 'delta_Z = baseline x coverage x FSF'. ΔZ is the vertical component of the geomagnetic field, not the total magnitude (ΔB). For the self-contradiction argument this distinction is irrelevant — the version-instability point is about FSF, not the kind of field. But a careful dome defender could pick at this: 'the review repeatedly says ΔB when the dome explicitly predicts ΔZ — what else did they get wrong?' Pre-existing — NOT introduced by EXP-266.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'ΔB =' with 'ΔZ =' (matching the dome publication). Also replace any 'ΔB' references in WIN-044 detail_evidence with 'ΔZ'. One-character substitution; same anchor verification protocol as EXP-266. Could be an EXP-267-style follow-up or rolled into the unit fix above as a single ~3-substring patch.

holes_found.description: 'purely geometric' mildly overstates FSF independence from physical parameters. H(r) under V51 is '8537 × exp(-r/8619)' with parameters chosen to fit the dome's refraction model n(r); the FSF ratio cancels much (but not all) of this dependence — the ratio is '(H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU)', which removes any pre-factor and the slope but leaves the exponential's curvature dependence on the same n(r) profile through r itself (since station radii are themselves Polaris-back-computed under n(r) per Section 1.8). The Section 1.8 cross-reference makes the parasitic-on-n(r) point, but only after 'purely geometric' has already been asserted in the paragraph above. A defender could ask: 'if the formula is purely geometric, how does Section 1.8 make it parasitic on n(r)?' The two statements are reconcilable but not obviously so to a casual reader.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider softening to 'formally geometric' or 'algebraically geometric' — the technical reader who follows the Section 1.8 link will understand the n(r) parasitism is via the station-radius pipeline, not the FSF formula itself. Alternatively, add a one-clause caveat: 'purely geometric (in the formula itself; the station radii it consumes are Polaris-derived through n(r), see Section 1.8)'. Optional; not a verdict-mover.

stronger_arguments.description: The version-instability argument is stronger when the magnitude of the parameter change is named. Between V50.6 and V51, H(r) was changed from an exponential profile to an ellipse profile (different functional form, not just different coefficients). A defender can dismiss 'parameter values changed' as routine model refinement, but 'the functional form of H(r) changed between versions' is a much harder thing to reconcile with the detail_claim of 'derived from V12 geometry'. The current detail_evidence mentions both 'ellipse H(r)' and 'exponential H(r)' but does not explicitly call out 'the functional form itself was changed'.

The 28% number could be sharpened to two-decimal precision: HAD-V50.6 / HAD-V51-published = 1.89/1.471 = 1.285, i.e. a 28.5% relative change. Detail_evidence says '28%' and detail_verdict_text says '28%'; both round the same direction so they agree, but '28.5%' is the auditable number. Minor; current presentation is fine for prose.

kernel_of_truth.description: The FSF formula is a legitimate algebraic object: given an H(r) profile and station radii, it produces a single ratio per station in a well-defined way. The intuition that eclipse-day ΔZ might vary with station position is empirically grounded — Sq current suppression and conductivity gradients do produce station-to-station amplitude variation in real eclipse datasets. EXP-266 is a clean editorial fix that removes a publication-defect without touching any of the substantive argument structure.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Being well-defined says nothing about being uniquely determined by V12 geometry. The version-instability test shows the same formula with V51 inputs and V50.6 inputs produces materially different per-station outputs at the same station — meaning either (a) the V12 derivation does not pin down the inputs and the formula is parameter-fit at each version, or (b) at most one of {V50.6, V51} matches the V12 derivation and the other is mis-labeled. Either reading contradicts the detail_claim. The empirical-reasonableness of station-amplitude variation also does not save the claim: standard geomagnetic models predict similar structure for different reasons, so the dome's prediction competes with rather than fills a gap in standard physics.

deeper_analysis:
EXP-266 is the smallest possible editorial patch — 7 characters out of 2197 — and the curmudgeon role here is to verify (a) the patch achieved its stated effect, (b) it did not perturb the substantive argument, and (c) no scope-leak occurred. Verifying (a): the substring 'nature.stency.

' is no longer present in WIN-044 detail_evidence; 'stency' now appears only as part of 'consistency' in the lead clause ('internal algebraic consistency'). The full wins.json 'stency' count is 8 occurrences, all matching 'consistency' — confirming no orphan fragment remains anywhere. Verifying (b): finding, detail_verdict_text, both TLDRs, and code_analysis are byte-identical to the pre-patch state per the analyst dry-run report (length delta − 7 chars matches expected). Verifying (c): no other field touched. The patch is clean. The remaining minor findings (unit inconsistency, ΔB vs ΔZ, 'purely geometric' framing) are pre-existing — they were present in the c4 detail_evidence and were not the EXP-266 target. They are worth flagging here as a punch list for a future editorial pass (perhaps grouped as a single small EXP), but none rise to verdict-mover or major-rewrite status; the Self-Contradicted verdict and the version-instability narrative are intact and well-supported. From the dome-defender side: the strongest counterargument I can construct is 'your ΔB-vs-ΔZ slip suggests you do not actually understand the dome publication well enough to claim self-contradiction.' That has rhetorical force but is empty on the merits — the formula applies identically to ΔZ as to any field component, and the version-instability test does not depend on which field component is being predicted. We should fix the transliteration nonetheless to deny the rhetorical opening. Recommended action 'no_change' reflects that the EXP-266 patch itself needs nothing more; the minor pre-existing findings can be queued as a separate small editorial EXP if the operator chooses, but they do not block close-out of queue_id 276.

citations_failed: raw-text/03-predictions.txt line 21 publishes the formula as 'delta_Z = baseline x coverage x FSF' — detail_evidence transliterates as 'ΔB = baseline × coverage × FSF'. The dome publishes a Z-component prediction; calling it ΔB is imprecise. Flagged as minor; pre-existing (not introduced by EXP-266).
citations_unchecked: monitor.py Domain 10 line 515 — raw-text/monitor.py is not present in the current clone (raw-text/ contains only 01-home.txt through 08-live-power.txt as of this run). The line-515 reference cannot be verified from the workspace. This is a separate concern from the EXP-266 patch and was already flagged in c4.
INTERMAGNET observatory data — observations not yet available (eclipse 2026-08-12)
